title: Trump Extends Ceasefire Indefinitely — Pending Iran Submitting 'Unified Proposal'; Vance Cancels Islamabad Trip
detail: The US-Iran ceasefire, originally set to expire April 22 (Day 54), did not lapse — President Trump extended it indefinitely in a social media post on April 21, just hours before the deadline. Trump wrote: 'Based on the fact that the government of Iran is seriously fractured...we have been asked to hold our attack. I have therefore directed our Military to continue the Blockade and, in all other respects, remain ready and able, and will therefore extend the Ceasefire until such time as their proposal is submitted, and discussions are concluded, one way or the other.' The extension came at the personal request of Pakistani PM Shehbaz Sharif and Field Marshal Asim Munir. Critically, the US naval blockade of Iranian ports remained fully in force. VP JD Vance's planned second round of Islamabad talks was canceled after it became clear Iran would not send a delegation. The New York Times first reported Vance's trip was 'on hold.' Trump gave Iran 'days — not weeks' to end its internal power struggle and return to talks, citing reports of a total breakdown in communication between Iran's civilian negotiators and Supreme Leader Mojtaba Khamenei, with IRGC hardliners under Gen. Ahmad Vahidi effectively sidelining diplomats.

media.caption: Trump extends Iran ceasefire indefinitely citing Iran's 'fractured' government; Vance cancels Islamabad trip

title: IRGC Seizes Two Cargo Ships in Strait of Hormuz — MSC Francesca and Epaminondas
detail: Hours after Trump's ceasefire extension was announced, Iran's Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps Navy seized two cargo vessels in the Strait of Hormuz on April 22 in what appeared to be a direct retaliation for the USS Spruance's seizure of the MV Touska on April 20. The IRGC confirmed boarding and seizing the MSC Francesca and the Epaminondas, transferring both to Iranian territorial waters 'to examine cargo and documents.' Iranian officials claimed both vessels had operated without required authorization and had tampered with navigation systems. Iranian state media also claimed the MSC Francesca had links to Israel. The seizures sent Brent crude spiking intraday toward $99-100/bbl before pulling back slightly after the ceasefire extension was digested by markets. Iran's FM Araghchi said Iran 'knows how to neutralize restrictions, how to defend its interests, and how to resist bullying,' calling the US naval blockade 'an act of war.' The dual ship seizures raised the specter of tit-for-tat maritime escalation and further complicated the path to resumed negotiations.

media.caption: IRGC seizes MSC Francesca and Epaminondas in Strait of Hormuz as ceasefire extension takes effect

title: Iranian Gunboats Fire on Container Ship 'Euphoria'; Vessel Runs Aground on Iranian Coast
detail: Alongside the seizures of MSC Francesca and Epaminondas, Iranian IRGC gunboats fired on a third vessel — the container ship Euphoria — in the Strait of Hormuz on April 22. The Euphoria sustained fire damage and became stranded near the Iranian coastline after being struck. Iranian gunboats also reportedly fired on at least one additional container ship near the strait. The multi-vessel action marked the most significant IRGC naval confrontation since the start of the conflict and demonstrated Iran's willingness to escalate maritime operations even amid the ceasefire extension on land. US News/AP confirmed Iranian forces fired on container ships in the Strait, complicating efforts to resume peace talks. The Washington Times reported Iran fired on three ships in total, with the Euphoria incident being the most serious given the vessel running aground. No crew casualties were immediately confirmed.

media.caption: Iranian gunboats fire on container ship Euphoria in Strait of Hormuz; vessel runs aground on Iranian coast

title: Brent Crude Spikes Near $100/bbl as IRGC Seizes Ships; Markets Whipsaw on Ceasefire Extension
detail: Global oil markets whipsawed sharply on April 22. Brent crude spiked toward $99/barrel — briefly approaching $100 — after IRGC gunboats seized the MSC Francesca and Epaminondas and fired on the Euphoria in the Strait of Hormuz. The spike was partially tempered after markets absorbed Trump's open-ended ceasefire extension, with Brent pulling back to around $95-99/bbl in volatile trading. WTI rose to approximately $90.86/barrel. CNBC reported gold also rose as 'oil weakened' after the ceasefire extension, as safe-haven flows competed with energy market jitters. Iranian state media noted Iran's estimated daily financial losses from the US blockade had reached ~$500 million/day. US Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent warned of targeting Iran's 'primary revenue lifelines' with new sanctions. The US Treasury also announced sanctions on 14 individuals, entities, and aircraft for procuring and transporting weapons for Iran.

media.caption: Oil market whipsaws — Brent near $100 on IRGC ship seizures, then pulling back after ceasefire extension

title: US Officials Report 'Absolute Fracture' Inside Iran; IRGC Hardliners Block Civilian Negotiators
detail: American officials disclosed on April 22 that a fundamental breakdown had occurred inside Iran's leadership, with IRGC hardliners under Gen. Ahmad Vahidi effectively sidelining civilian negotiators and blocking any deal. US officials described 'an absolute fracture inside Iran between the negotiators and the military — with neither side having access to the supreme leader, who is not responsive.' Supreme Leader Mojtaba Khamenei, who replaced Ali Khamenei after his death in February 2026, was described as 'largely silent and inaccessible' — even senior civilian Iranian negotiators lacked direct contact with him. Trump's ceasefire extension was explicitly framed around this paralysis: 'Based on the fact that the government of Iran is seriously fractured.' Time magazine reported on the power vacuum at Iran's top, with IRGC commanders exercising de facto military authority. Iran's civilian Foreign Minister Araghchi continued to signal openness to dialogue in principle, but IRGC hardliners had already rejected terms that Araghchi's team had discussed in the first Islamabad round. US officials gave Iran 'days, not weeks' to resolve the internal standoff and submit a unified proposal for negotiation to resume.

media.caption: US officials report absolute fracture inside Iran as IRGC hardliners block civilian negotiators from reaching deal
